task_goal: Extract Characters, Concepts, and Objects that exist in the narrative world and are relevant to the story. Entities must be grounded in the text and usable as structured nodes in a narrative knowledge graph.
global_constraints: Do NOT invent entities that are not explicitly grounded in the text.
Exclude purely cinematic devices or formatting artifacts.
Prefer stable, canonical names; avoid duplicate variants unless the text clearly distinguishes them.
Output JSON only. Do NOT include commentary or extra text.

types.description: A concrete individual entity that participates in the narrative (human or anthropomorphized agent).
naming_rules: Use a clean, descriptive entity name.
Avoid verb phrases and full sentences.
Do NOT output purely generic placeholders as names unless uniquely individuated by context.

scope_rules.global: Use global only if the character is a stable, re-identifiable in-world referent that plausibly recurs across scenes or drives ongoing plot developments (named or uniquely identifiable recurring individual).
scope_rules.local: Use local if the character is scene-bound, generic, deictic, or not uniquely resolvable beyond the immediate context (e.g., “Narrator”, “someone”, “a man”, “the driver”), unless clearly the same recurring individual. If uncertain, prefer local.

exclusions: Abstract roles without individuality (e.g., “a crowd”, “people in general”)
Organizations or institutions (these are Concepts)
Generic references without identity unless clearly individuated by context

types.description: An abstract, collective, or institutional entity such as an organization, faction, ideology, system, or social group that frames or constrains the narrative.
naming_rules: Use a clear noun phrase naming the institution/system/group/idea.
Do NOT output specific meeting/session instances as Concepts (those are Occasions).

scope_rules.global: Use global if the concept represents a persistent organization, system, faction, ideology, or framework that plausibly persists beyond a single scene.
scope_rules.local: Use local if the concept is a one-off, scene-specific abstraction or label not clearly persistent. If uncertain, prefer local.

exclusions: Individual people (Characters)
Physical locations (Locations)
Physical objects (Objects)
Specific instances of meetings/gatherings (Occasions)

types.description: A concrete physical item that can be held, used, hidden, damaged, or exchanged, and is narratively relevant.
naming_rules: Use a descriptive noun phrase.
Avoid overly generic labels when the text provides specificity.
Do NOT treat places/architectural spaces as objects (those are Locations).

scope_rules.global: Use global if the object is likely to recur across scenes or is clearly a persistent plot-relevant artifact (uniquely identifiable or repeatedly referenced).
scope_rules.local: Use local if the object is scene-specific or only relevant within a short context. If uncertain, prefer local.

exclusions: Abstract ideas or institutions (Concept)
Places or settings (Location)
Natural substances mentioned only as background unless narratively relevant

task_goal: Extract Events and Occasions from the narrative text. Events are atomic narrative occurrences; Occasions are organized activity containers that may include multiple Events.

types.description: A concrete, atomic occurrence in the narrative world describable as who did what to whom/what, when/where, and with what consequence. Includes actions, meaningful speech acts, decisions, and clear changes of state (including location transitions).

scope_rules.local: Events are scene-anchored by default. Use local unless explicitly instructed otherwise.

naming_rules: Name must be a descriptive, content-centered natural language phrase.
Do NOT use a single character name, a single verb, a time point alone, a location alone, or an object alone.
Avoid overly short or vague phrases.
granularity_rules: Extract atomic events only; split multiple distinct actions/state changes into separate Events.
If any character(s) are present, extract at least one Event involving them whenever the text supports it, unless there is insufficient evidence to form a valid Event.
exclusions: Narrative or cinematic devices (montage, camera movement, scene cuts)
Pure descriptions without action, state change, or a recognizable organized happening
Isolated elements such as character names, time expressions, locations, or objects without event context
Abstract institutions or organizations themselves unless describing a specific meeting/session instance (Occasion)

types.description: A structured, socially or institutionally recognized activity or gathering that takes place in the narrative. It is an organized event container that can be attended/hosted/participated in, may span a period of time, and may contain multiple atomic Events.

scope_rules.global: Occasions are treated as globally scoped containers by default.
scope_rules.local: Use local only if explicitly instructed otherwise.

naming_rules: Prefer a concrete instance name if available (e.g., 'Alcoholics Anonymous meeting').
If unnamed, use a grounded descriptive label (e.g., 'local AA meeting', 'emergency briefing').
Name must not be overly short or vague.
granularity_rules: Extract Occasions only when the organized gathering/activity itself is narratively relevant.
Do not replace atomic Events with an Occasion; extract both when appropriate.
exclusions: Cinematic devices or scene formatting
Abstract institutions/organizations themselves without a concrete occurrence instance

task_goal: Extract time expressions and location mentions that can anchor events in a scene. These anchors should be usable as structured nodes, not as events.
global_constraints: Do NOT invent times or locations not supported by the text.
Do NOT extract cinematic directions (wide shot, cut to exterior).
Do NOT extract abstract/metaphorical places (e.g., 'in his mind').
Do NOT extract deictic-only anchors as names (e.g., 'now', 'here').
Output JSON only. Do NOT include commentary or extra text.

types.description: A standalone temporal expression that indicates when something happens, as a time point or short, specific temporal reference (clock time, time of day, date-like reference, or short relative reference).
naming_rules: Extract the smallest time phrase that remains meaningful as an anchor.
Do NOT output bare deictic tokens as name (e.g., now, right now, currently, at present, these days).

scope_rules.global: Calendar-anchored expressions such as explicit year/month/day/weekday or explicit date-time.
scope_rules.local: Relative or scene-dependent anchors (later, that night, moments later). If ambiguous, prefer local.

exclusions: Events phrased as time
Broad eras or long spans not scene-anchoring (during the war, in his childhood, in the 1990s)
Purely atmospheric phrases without a temporal anchor
Deictic-only references without temporal content (now/right now/currently)

types.description: A physical setting where events occur, expressed as a noun phrase (named place, specific setting, or interior sub-location).
naming_rules: The name MUST be a clean noun phrase with NO leading prepositions.
Avoid overly generic labels when the text provides specificity.
The name MUST NOT be a pure deictic word or placeholder without a concrete place noun (e.g., here, there, somewhere, this place).
If the text only says here/there but nearby context provides a concrete place noun, extract that noun phrase instead; otherwise skip.

scope_rules.global: Named or uniquely identifying places (proper names, specific institutions/buildings/facilities, landmarks).
scope_rules.local: Generic or scene-relative settings (hallway, car interior, basement) unless uniquely identified. If ambiguous, prefer local.

exclusions: Cinematic directions or shot labels
Abstract or metaphorical places
Fragmentary tokens without narrative meaning
Deictic-only placeholders as names (here/there/nearby/outside/inside without a noun anchor)
